title: Ethiopia needs more than an election to calm internal and regional conflict
gist: Ethiopia's 1 June election is a tightly managed elite bargain rather than a real contest, unfolding as the Pretoria Agreement with the TPLF unravels in Tigray and rival regional blocs (Ethiopia/UAE/Israel vs. TPLF/Eritrea/SAF backed by Saudi Arabia/Egypt/Turkey) risk drawing the country back into a wider war that could spill into Eritrea and Sudan.

themes.theme: Election as elite bargain, not real contest

insights.claim: Ethiopia's 1 June election is likely to be the least competitive of the seven national elections held since multiparty democracy was introduced in 1991, functioning to reinforce incumbent power rather than offer real political choice.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Treat the vote as a legitimacy exercise for the ruling PP rather than a signal of genuine political change or stability.

insights.claim: Despite headline numbers suggesting competition (50+ million registered voters, 11,000+ candidates from 47 parties), some opposition parties are reportedly aligned with the government and negotiating post-election power-sharing arrangements, while tactically not contesting certain seats.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Large voter/candidate counts are not a reliable proxy for genuine electoral competitiveness in Ethiopia.
insights.locator: On the surface, the numbers suggest a competitive electoral process

insights.claim: An electoral victory could give Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed a route to constitutional reforms strengthening central authority, including creating an executive presidency and altering Ethiopia's ethnic federal structure.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Watch post-election constitutional moves as the real signal of Abiy's centralization intentions, not the vote itself.
insights.locator: Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed is seeking an election victory

insights.claim: Armed opposition groups reject the election's legitimacy: the Fano group in Amhara has warned it will treat election participants as enemies of the Amhara people, and the Oromo Liberation Army has escalated attacks in Oromia since federal forces were redeployed toward Tigray in February.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Redeployment of federal troops toward Tigray is creating a security vacuum that armed groups elsewhere are actively exploiting.
insights.locator: The Fano armed group in the Amhara region

themes.theme: Tigray and the collapse of the Pretoria Agreement

insights.claim: The 2022 Pretoria Agreement between the federal government and the TPLF has unravelled: the TPLF reconstituted its pre-war legislative council and elected party chairman Debretsion Gebremichael as regional president after the federal government unilaterally renewed the term of interim administration president Tadesse Worede.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A negotiated power-sharing framework that ended a devastating 2020-2022 war is breaking down, raising direct risk of renewed conflict.

insights.claim: Severe fuel shortages resulting from the Iran war are constraining Abiy's appetite to launch a drawn-out military campaign in Tigray before the election, even though he has already moved federal forces north.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: An external shock (Iran war fuel disruption) is acting as an unintended brake on Ethiopian military escalation timing.
insights.locator: Yet Abiy may not want to rush an armed response

insights.claim: The federal government is pursuing a dual strategy against the TPLF short of open war: economically squeezing Tigray while tacitly backing rival Tigrayan opposition, including Abiy advisor Getachew Reda's Simret party, to erode TPLF dominance.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A slower, non-military delegitimization strategy is an alternative escalation path to watch alongside the military option.
insights.locator: Amid a decline in relations since the Pretoria Agreement

insights.claim: A regional 'enemy of my enemy is my friend' logic has aligned the TPLF with Eritrea and Sudan's Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF), all of which have strained relations with Addis Ababa, while this bloc is more broadly aligned with Saudi Arabia, Egypt and Turkey against the UAE and Israel, who back Ethiopia and Somaliland.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Ethiopia's internal conflict is nested inside a wider Red Sea/Horn of Africa proxy contest, so outcomes will be shaped by external patrons, not just domestic actors.

insights.claim: A relapse into conflict in Tigray would not stay confined to Ethiopia and would likely trigger a wider regional conflagration drawing in Eritrea, Sudan and their respective allies.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Any renewed Tigray war is a regional-spillover risk, not a contained civil conflict, relevant to Horn of Africa and Red Sea shipping stability.
insights.locator: A relapse into conflict in Tigray will not be confined

insights.claim: In response to the TPLF-Eritrea-SAF 'Tsimdo' alliance, Ethiopia has reportedly facilitated support to the SAF's enemies in Sudan, the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) and SPLM-N; Reuters and Yale's Humanitarian Research Lab report Ethiopia hosts an RSF training camp in Benishangul-Gumuz allegedly financed and supported by the UAE, which Abu Dhabi denies.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Ethiopia's involvement in the Sudan conflict as an RSF-aligned actor widens the risk that any Tigray escalation triggers direct Sudan-Ethiopia friction.
insights.locator: According to reports from Reuters and Yale's Humanitarian Research Lab

insights.claim: The SAF has separately accused Ethiopia of allowing drones targeting Sudan to be launched from Ethiopian territory, an allegation Ethiopia denies.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: An additional unconfirmed friction point that could escalate Ethiopia-Sudan tensions independent of the Tigray dynamic.
insights.locator: The SAF also accused Ethiopia of allowing the launch of drones

insights.claim: The African Union has re-appointed former Nigerian president Olusegun Obasanjo as a regional envoy to rebuild mediation channels, and should build a credible team alongside existing envoy former Tanzanian president Jakaya Kikwete, potentially adding former Kenyan president Uhuru Kenyatta and South Africa's Phumzile Mlambo-Ngcuka.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Track AU mediation appointments as the leading indicator of whether renewed Tigray war can be averted diplomatically.

insights.claim: Effective mediation needs to be bolstered by coordinated efforts from major international actors with a stake in regional stability: the US, EU, China, Saudi Arabia, Turkey and the UAE.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Absent great-power and regional-power coordination, AU-led mediation alone is unlikely to prevent escalation.
insights.locator: Crucially, this mediation need to be bolstered
